title: Statistics & Data
blurb: Mean vs median, standard deviation, z-scores, and the normal curve that grades on it.

lessons.title: Mean vs. Median
lessons.concreteComparison: Nine people in a cafe earn ordinary salaries; a billionaire walks in. The AVERAGE income jumps to nine digits — but nobody in the room got richer. The **mean** follows every dollar; the **median** (the middle person) barely notices. Choosing the right 'center' is the first honest decision in statistics, and headline-writers exploit the wrong one daily.
lessons.objective: Compute mean and median, know how outliers pull the mean but not the median, and pick the appropriate center.
concept: **Mean**: total ÷ count. Salaries $30, 35, 40, 55$ (thousands): mean $= \tfrac{160}{4} = 40$.
**Median**: the middle value of the SORTED list — or the mean of the middle two when the count is even. Same data: median $= \tfrac{35+40}{2} = 37.5$.
**Outliers pull the mean**, not the median: append a 240 to that list and the mean leaps to $80$ while the median only slides to $40$. One wild value, two very different stories.
Choosing: symmetric, well-behaved data → mean (it uses every value). Skewed data or outliers (incomes, house prices) → median. When a report says 'average', always ask WHICH one.
lessons.keyIdea: Mean = total/count and follows every value; median = the sorted middle and shrugs at outliers. Skewed data wants the median.

facts.title: Outlier law
facts.latex: \text{outliers drag the mean, not the median}

workedExamples.statement: Find the mean and median of $30, 35, 40, 55$.
Append $240$ to that list. New mean and median?
workedExamples.solution: Mean $\tfrac{400}{5} = 80$; median (middle of 5) $= 40$. The outlier moved the mean $40$ points and the median $2.5$.
Both $80$ — symmetric data agrees with itself.

commonMistakes.text: Taking the median without sorting first.
commonMistakes.correction: The median of $9, 2, 7$ is 7 (sorted: $2, 7, 9$), not 2. Sort, THEN find the middle.

commonMistakes.text: Reporting the mean for skewed data as 'the typical value'.
commonMistakes.correction: Mean income in the billionaire cafe is millions; the TYPICAL person earns 40k. For skewed data the median is the honest 'typical'.

steps.title: Two 'averages', two stories
beats: Nine cafe-goers, ordinary incomes. One billionaire enters.
MEAN income: nine digits. Nobody's richer.
MEDIAN income: the middle person. Barely moves.
'Average' hides a choice. Statistics starts by exposing it.

steps.title: Compute both
beats: Mean: add everything, divide by the count.
Median: SORT, take the middle (or middle-two average).
Close here — because no outlier. Yet.

steps.title: Sort first!
steps.explanation: Sorted: $3, 5, 8, 10, 12$ — middle is 8. ($7.6$ is the MEAN.)

steps.title: Drag test
beats: Mean: $40 \to 80$. Doubled by one guest.
Median: $37.5 \to 40$. Shrugged.
The mean follows every dollar; the median follows the crowd.

steps.title: House prices
steps.prompt: A town's house prices are mostly ~50M with a few palaces. The honest 'typical price' is…
steps.explanation: Right-skewed data: palaces drag the mean up. The median stays with the typical house — which is why real-estate reports quote it.

examples.prompt: Five games, points: $18, 22, 20, 24, 96$. Mean, median, and which represents a typical game?
steps: One blowout dragged the mean 14 points above every normal game: median.

examples.prompt: A class of 9 averages 70 on a quiz. A 10th student scores 100. New mean?

steps.title: Honest centers
steps.intro: Sort for medians; totals for means.

problems.prompt: Which measure changes MOST when 1000 joins $10, 20, 30$?
problems.explanation: Mean: $20 \to 265$. Median: $20 \to 25$. Outliers own the mean.
When the two centers agree, nothing is dragging one of them — the data is balanced.

steps.title: The 'average person' has fewer than 2 legs
steps.body: Mean legs per human is about 1.999 (amputees pull it below 2) — so almost everyone is 'above average'. The joke is a real lesson: for lopsided data the mean describes NOBODY. Statisticians keep it framed on office walls as a warning against thoughtless averaging.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Mean = total ÷ count; median = sorted middle.
Outliers drag the mean and barely touch the median.
Skewed data → median; symmetric data → mean.

lessons.title: Spread & Standard Deviation
lessons.concreteComparison: Two archers both average a bullseye. One's arrows cluster in the gold; the other's spray the whole target. Same center — completely different shooters. **Spread** is the missing half of every data story, and the **standard deviation** is its ruler: the typical distance from the mean.
lessons.objective: Compute range and (population) standard deviation, and interpret SD as typical distance from the mean.
concept: **Range** = max − min: quick but fragile (two values decide it). The serious tool is the standard deviation.

**Standard deviation** recipe: (1) find the mean; (2) square each value's deviation from it; (3) average those squares (the **variance**); (4) square-root back. For $2,4,4,4,5,5,7,9$: mean 5, squared deviations sum 32, variance 4, SD $= 2$.
Why square? Deviations alone cancel to zero (the mean is the balance point). Squaring keeps distances positive — then the final root returns to the original units.
Interpretation: SD ≈ the TYPICAL miss. SD 2 means values typically sit about 2 from the mean. Tight archer: small SD. Spray archer: big SD. Same mean either way.
lessons.keyIdea: SD = √(average of squared deviations): the typical distance from the mean. Small SD = consistent; large = scattered.

facts.title: Reading it
facts.explanation: Distance from the mean, in original units.

workedExamples.statement: Two archers, five shots' distances from center: A: $1,1,2,2,4$; B: $0,0,1,4,5$. Range of each?
workedExamples.solution: A: $4-1 = 3$; B: $5-0 = 5$. B is streakier — brilliant and terrible.

workedExamples.statement: Why do raw deviations always sum to zero? Show it for $2, 5, 8$ (mean 5).
workedExamples.solution: $(2-5) + (5-5) + (8-5) = -3 + 0 + 3 = 0$ — the mean balances; that's why we square.

commonMistakes.text: Averaging the raw deviations (getting 0) and reporting 'no spread'.
commonMistakes.correction: Deviations ALWAYS cancel — the mean is the balance point. Square first, average, then root: that's the whole reason the recipe exists.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting the final square root and reporting the variance.
commonMistakes.correction: Variance 4 means SD 2 — the variance is in SQUARED units (points², cm²). Root back before interpreting.

tryIt.solution: First: SD 0 (no spread). Second: variance $\tfrac{50}{3}$ — clearly bigger.

steps.title: Why spread matters
beats: Two archers, both AVERAGE a bullseye.
One clusters in the gold. One sprays the target.
The mean can't tell them apart. Spread can.
Statistics needs both coordinates: center AND spread.

steps.title: Four steps to σ
beats: 1. Mean. 2. Square each deviation.
3. Average the squares (= variance).
4. Square-root back to real units.

steps.title: Balance point
steps.prompt: The raw deviations from the mean always sum to…
steps.explanation: The mean balances the data perfectly — that's its definition. Squaring rescues the information.

steps.title: The typical miss
beats: SD 2 (in points): values typically sit ~2 from the mean.
Quiz scores mean 70, SD 3: most scores near 67–73. Tight.
Mean 70, SD 15: scores everywhere. Loose.
One number, and you can picture the whole spray.

steps.title: Which is which?
steps.prompt: Factory A's bolts: SD 0.1 mm. Factory B's: SD 2 mm. The consistent factory is…
options: A — small SD means tight clustering
steps.explanation: Small SD = little typical deviation = consistency. (Precision manufacturing is a war against σ.)

steps.title: σ in action
steps.intro: Recipe start to finish; then interpret.

examples.prompt: Two routes to school, times: A: $20, 20, 21, 19, 20$; B: $14, 26, 18, 24, 18$. Both mean 20 — which route for exam day?
steps: Route A: nearly deterministic. Take A when it matters.

steps.title: Spread fluency
steps.intro: Squares, average, root — and read it.

problems.prompt: The variance of a dataset is 25. Its SD is…
Adding 10 to EVERY value does what to the SD?
problems.explanation: Shifting everyone equally moves the mean but not the distances from it. (Deviations are shift-proof.)

problems.prompt: A dataset with SD = 0 must be…
problems.explanation: Zero typical distance from the mean = nobody deviates = every value equal.

steps.title: The σ that finds planets
steps.body: Astronomers announce discoveries in sigmas: a '5-sigma' signal sits five SDs from noise — odds about 1 in 3.5 million of a fluke. The Higgs boson waited YEARS at 3–4 sigma before CERN would say 'discovery' at 5. Standard deviation isn't just spread; it's science's threshold for believing its own eyes.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Spread completes the story the mean starts.
σ: square deviations, average, root — the typical miss.
Small σ = consistent; σ = 0 = identical; shifts don't change it.

lessons.title: Z-Scores
lessons.concreteComparison: You scored 85 in math (class mean 70) and 78 in history (mean 70). Better at math? The math test had SD 15 — your 85 is one SD up. History's SD was 4 — your 78 is TWO SDs up. The **z-score** is the universal translator: distances measured in SDs, so any two performances, on any scales, become comparable.
lessons.objective: Compute $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, interpret sign and size, and use z-scores to compare across different scales.
concept: **Z-score**: $z = \tfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ — how many standard deviations $x$ sits from the mean. $x = 85$, $\mu = 70$, $\sigma = 15$: $z = 1$.
Sign reads direction: positive = above the mean, negative = below, $z = 0$ = exactly average. Size reads rarity: $|z| < 1$ is ordinary, $|z| > 2$ is notable, $|z| > 3$ is exceptional.
Cross-scale comparison: math $z = 1$ vs history $z = \tfrac{78-70}{4} = 2$ — the history result is the rarer feat, despite fewer raw points.
Backwards too: $x = \mu + z\sigma$. 'The cutoff is $z = 1.5$' with $\mu = 100, \sigma = 10$ means $x = 115$.
lessons.keyIdea: z = (x − μ)/σ: distance from the mean in SD units. Sign = direction, size = rarity; x = μ + zσ runs it backwards.

facts.title: Backwards
facts.explanation: From z back to the raw score.

workedExamples.statement: Math: 85 with $\mu = 70, \sigma = 15$. History: 78 with $\mu = 70, \sigma = 4$. Compute both z-scores.
workedExamples.solution: Math $z = 1$; history $z = 2$ — history was the stronger performance.

workedExamples.statement: Heights: $\mu = 170$ cm, $\sigma = 8$. Find $z$ for 154 cm and 186 cm.
A scholarship requires $z \ge 1.5$ on a test with $\mu = 200, \sigma = 20$. Minimum raw score?

commonMistakes.text: Comparing raw scores across different tests.
commonMistakes.correction: 85 > 78 in points, but the tests had different spreads: z-scores (1 vs 2) reverse the verdict. Raw points don't transfer between scales; sigmas do.

commonMistakes.text: Dropping the sign on below-mean values.
commonMistakes.correction: 154 cm has $z = -2$, not 2. The minus IS the information: two SDs BELOW. Direction matters as much as distance.

steps.title: The two-test puzzle
beats: Math 85 (mean 70). History 78 (mean 70).
More points in math — but was it BETTER?
Math's spread: σ = 15. History's: σ = 4.
Measure in SDs and the answer flips…

steps.title: z = (x − μ)/σ
beats: Math: $\tfrac{85-70}{15} = 1$ SD above.
History: $\tfrac{78-70}{4} = 2$ SDs above.
The history score is the rarer feat.
Any scale in, SD-units out — scores become comparable.

steps.title: Translate
steps.explanation: $\tfrac{64-80}{8} = -2$: two SDs BELOW. The sign carries the direction.

steps.title: Direction and rarity
beats: $z > 0$: above the mean. $z < 0$: below. $z = 0$: dead average.
$|z| < 1$: crowd territory (most data lives here).
$|z| > 2$: notable. $|z| > 3$: newspaper-worthy.
Next lesson attaches exact percentages to these zones.

steps.title: Cross-sport
steps.prompt: Runner A: 100m in z = −2.2 (fast = below mean time). Swimmer B: z = −1.1. The stronger performance vs their field is…
options: A — further below the mean time
steps.explanation: More SDs from the pack = rarer feat. Negative direction is GOOD here (faster than mean) — read sign against context.

examples.prompt: SAT-style: $\mu = 500, \sigma = 100$; score 650. IQ-style: $\mu = 100, \sigma = 15$; score 130. Which is rarer?
steps: The 130 IQ is the rarer result.

examples.prompt: A factory rejects bolts with $|z| > 2$; $\mu = 50.0$ mm, $\sigma = 0.2$. Acceptance range?

steps.title: SD-unit fluency
steps.intro: Sign, size, and the backwards formula.

problems.explanation: Rarity is $|z|$: 2.8 beats 1.9 regardless of direction.

problems.prompt: Your z on a test is exactly 0. You scored…
problems.explanation: $z = 0 \iff x = \mu$. (Zero POINTS would usually be a very negative z!)

steps.title: The z that grades the Olympics
steps.body: Decathlon scoring converts ten wildly different events — sprint seconds, javelin meters, jump heights — into points via formulas built on z-score logic: how far above the reference performance, in spread units? It's the only fair way to add a 100m dash to a shot put. Ten scales, one ruler.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Sign = direction from the mean; $|z|$ = rarity.
Cross-scale comparisons and cutoffs: translate, then judge.

lessons.title: The Normal Curve
lessons.concreteComparison: Measure a thousand people's heights and the histogram forms a shape nature loves: a symmetric bell, crowded in the middle, thinning to rare tails. Heights, measurement errors, test scores — the **normal curve** appears whenever many small independent effects add up. And it obeys one astonishingly useful rule of thumb: 68–95–99.7.
lessons.objective: Describe the normal distribution's shape and apply the empirical (68-95-99.7) rule to intervals around the mean.
concept: **Shape**: symmetric bell centered at $\mu$ — mean = median there. The SD $\sigma$ sets the width: the curve's 'shoulders' sit exactly at $\mu \pm \sigma$.
**The empirical rule**: about **68%** of data lies within 1 SD of the mean, **95%** within 2 SDs, **99.7%** within 3. (That's why $|z| > 2$ felt notable and $|z| > 3$ exceptional.)
Interval reading: heights $\mu = 170, \sigma = 8$ → 68% between 162 and 178; 95% between 154 and 186; almost everyone between 146 and 194.
Symmetry splits everything: outside $\pm 2$ SD lives $5\%$ — so $2.5\%$ in EACH tail. Above $\mu$: exactly $50\%$. Every empirical-rule question is adding and halving these zones.
lessons.keyIdea: The bell: symmetric around μ, width set by σ; 68% within 1σ, 95% within 2σ, 99.7% within 3σ — halve tails by symmetry.

workedExamples.statement: Heights: $\mu = 170$ cm, $\sigma = 8$. Between which heights do the middle 68% and 95% fall?
What percent of data lies ABOVE $\mu + 2\sigma$?
Scores: $\mu = 70, \sigma = 5$. What percent scored between 70 and 80?

commonMistakes.correction: The rule is a property of the NORMAL shape. Incomes (skewed) and dice sums (small-n) disobey it; check the shape before quoting the rule.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to halve for one-sided questions.
commonMistakes.correction: 'Within 1σ' is 68% — but 'ABOVE μ+σ' is $\tfrac{100-68}{2} = 16\%$, not 32%. Draw the bell; shade the region; halve tails.

steps.title: The bell appears
beats: Heights, lab-measurement errors, test scores…
Histogram enough of them: a symmetric bell.
Crowded middle, rare tails, mean = median at the peak.
It appears whenever MANY small effects add up.
Within $\mu \pm \sigma$: about 68% of everything.
Within $\mu \pm 2\sigma$: 95%.
Within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$: 99.7% — almost all.
Three numbers, memorized once, used forever.

steps.title: One-sided questions
beats: Outside $\pm 1\sigma$: 32% — so 16% per tail.
Outside $\pm 2\sigma$: 5% — so 2.5% per tail.
'Percent above $\mu + \sigma$?' → one tail → 16%.
Sketch the bell, shade, halve. Never guess.

steps.title: Above two sigma
steps.prompt: IQ-style scores: $\mu = 100, \sigma = 15$. About what percent score above 130?
steps.explanation: 130 is $+2\sigma$; beyond ±2σ is 5%, halved: 2.5% in the upper tail.

examples.prompt: Battery life: $\mu = 20$ h, $\sigma = 2$. What percent last between 16 and 24 hours? Under 14?
In a school of 1000 students (normal scores, $\mu = 70, \sigma = 5$): about how many score above 80?

problems.prompt: Percent of normal data within 3 SDs of the mean?
problems.explanation: Nearly all — but never quite 100: the tails go on forever, thinly.

problems.prompt: Percent BELOW the mean of any normal distribution?
problems.explanation: Perfect symmetry: half below, half above. (Mean = median on a bell.)
0 to $+1\sigma$ is half of the 68% band.

steps.title: The curve on old money
steps.body: Germany's 10-Deutschmark note carried Gauss's portrait AND the bell curve with its equation — the only banknote to feature a probability density. Fitting: Gauss derived it correcting astronomical measurement errors, and for two centuries since, 'errors behave normally' has been science's default assumption. A curve so trusted they printed money on it.

steps.title: What you learned
points: The bell: symmetric at $\mu$, shoulders at $\mu \pm \sigma$.
One-sided questions: sketch, shade, halve the leftover.

lessons.title: Working the Normal Model
lessons.concreteComparison: The last two lessons snap together: z-scores locate a value on the bell, and the empirical rule prices the real estate. 'How rare is a 186 cm height?' becomes 'z = 2 → top 2.5%'. This lesson is the full workflow — raw score → z → bell zone → percent — the exact machine behind standardized-test percentiles.
lessons.objective: Chain z-scores with the empirical rule to find percentages and percentiles for normal data, in both directions.
concept: The workflow: raw $x$ → $z = \tfrac{x-\mu}{\sigma}$ → place on the bell → read the zone's percent. Heights $\mu=170, \sigma=8$: is 186 rare? $z = 2$ → above it: 2.5%.
**Percentile** = percent BELOW you. $z = 1$: $50 + 34 = 84$th percentile. $z = -2$: $2.5$th. $z = 0$: the 50th, dead middle.
Reverse workflow: 'What score is the 97.5th percentile?' → that's $z = 2$ → $x = \mu + 2\sigma$. Percentile → z → raw.
Between-two-values questions stack zones: from $z = -1$ to $z = 2$ is $34 + 47.5 = 81.5\%$. Always sketch; the bell does the bookkeeping.
lessons.keyIdea: Raw → z → bell zone → percent (and reverse). Percentile = percent below; z = 1, 2 map to the 84th, 97.5th.

workedExamples.statement: Test $\mu = 70, \sigma = 5$: what score marks the 97.5th percentile?
Same test: what fraction scores between 65 and 80?

commonMistakes.correction: The 84th percentile means 84% of people scored BELOW you — not that you scored 84%. A 60% score can be the 99th percentile on a brutal test.

commonMistakes.text: Adding zone percentages without a sketch.
commonMistakes.correction: From $z=-1$ to $z=2$: it's 34 + 47.5, not 68 + 95. Each side of the mean contributes its HALF-band. The sketch prevents double-counting.

tryIt.statement: What z marks the 84th percentile, and the raw score when $\mu = 60, \sigma = 10$?

steps.title: The full machine
beats: Lesson 3: z locates you on the bell.
Lesson 4: the rule prices each zone.
Chain them: raw → z → zone → percent.
That chain IS how percentiles get computed.

steps.title: Percentile anchors
beats: $z = -2$: 2.5th percentile. $z = -1$: 16th.
$z = 0$: 50th (the median).
Five anchors cover most exam questions cold.

steps.title: Run the chain
steps.prompt: $\mu = 170, \sigma = 8$: a height of 162 cm is at the…
steps.explanation: $z = -1$ → 50 − 34 = 16% below... wait: BELOW $-1σ$ is 16%, so 16th percentile.

steps.title: Percentile → score
beats: 'Top 2.5% qualify' = 97.5th percentile = $z = 2$.
$x = \mu + 2\sigma$. Done.
Cutoffs, admissions bars, clinical thresholds:
all set by driving the chain backwards.

steps.title: Find the cutoff
steps.prompt: Reaction times $\mu = 250$ ms, $\sigma = 30$. The fastest 16% beat…

steps.title: Percentile factory
steps.intro: Chain forward; chain backward; stack zones.

examples.prompt: Same exam: what range of scores covers the middle 68% of students?
Chip factory: lifetimes $\mu = 5000$ h, $\sigma = 400$. Warranty covers the bottom 2.5%. Warranty threshold?

problems.explanation: The mean splits the bell in half — median and mean coincide.

problems.prompt: From the 16th to the 84th percentile lies what percent of the data?

steps.title: Growth charts are percentile machines
steps.body: A pediatrician plotting a baby on the '75th percentile for weight' is running this exact chain against reference data from millions of children. The curved lines on the chart ARE constant-z curves aging through time. Parents worldwide read normal-model output weekly without knowing its name.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Chain: raw → z → zone → percent — and in reverse for cutoffs.
Sketch the bell; stack half-bands; never double-count.

lessons.title: How Data Lies
lessons.concreteComparison: Ice-cream sales and drowning deaths rise together every summer — does dessert cause drowning? A supplement ad's 'up to 90% improvement!' — measured how, on whom, versus what? Statistics is powerful enough that its misuses are powerful too. The course ends with the defense kit: the five questions that expose bad data before it fools you.
lessons.objective: Spot correlation-vs-causation errors, biased samples, misleading graphs, and cherry-picked statistics.
concept: **Correlation ≠ causation**: ice cream and drownings share a cause (summer), not a causal link. Ask: could a third factor drive both? Could the arrow point backwards?
**Biased samples**: polling gym-goers about exercise, or your own followers about your popularity, measures the SAMPLE, not the world. Ask: who was asked — and who COULDN'T be in the sample?
**Misleading pictures**: a y-axis starting at 98 turns a 1% wiggle into a cliff; a truncated timeline hides the trend that embarrasses. Ask: where do the axes start, and what's cropped?
**Cherry-picked centers**: 'average salary 90k' (the mean, dragged by executives) vs the 45k median. Ask: which center, and what's the spread? — the whole unit, weaponized as skepticism.
lessons.keyIdea: Four traps: third factors, biased samples, cropped axes, and convenient averages. Five questions expose them all.

facts.title: The mantra
facts.explanation: Shared causes and reversed arrows lurk.

facts.title: The defense
facts.latex: \text{who? how many? which center? what axes? vs what?}

workedExamples.statement: Cities with more firefighters have more fires. Do firefighters cause fires?
workedExamples.solution: No — city SIZE drives both. Per-capita rates (dividing by population) remove the third factor.

workedExamples.statement: A company: salaries 40, 42, 44, 46, 228 (thousands). The recruiter says 'average = 80'. Check both centers.
workedExamples.solution: Mean $= \tfrac{400}{5} = 80$ ✓ technically true; median $= 44$ — the honest 'typical'. Ask which center!

workedExamples.statement: A graph shows sales 'doubling' — bars of height 100 and 102 on an axis from 98 to 104. What really happened?
workedExamples.solution: Growth of $\tfrac{2}{100} = 2\%$. The cropped axis made 2% LOOK like 100%. Read axes before bars.

commonMistakes.text: Accepting 'studies show a link' as 'X causes Y'.
commonMistakes.correction: A link is a correlation. Before believing causation, hunt third factors and reversed arrows — most headlines skip both.

commonMistakes.text: Trusting a poll because its sample is LARGE.
commonMistakes.correction: A million biased answers beat nobody: the 1936 Literary Digest polled 2.4 MILLION people (by car/phone lists — wealthy owners) and called the US election wrong by 19 points. Representative beats big.

tryIt.statement: Shoe size correlates with reading ability in children. Third factor?
tryIt.solution: AGE — older kids have bigger feet and read better.

tryIt.statement: 'Average' bonus is quoted as 12k; the median is 2k. What does the gap tell you?
tryIt.solution: Heavy right skew — a few giant bonuses drag the mean; the typical worker gets ~2k.

steps.title: Correlation's oldest trick
beats: Ice-cream sales up → drownings up. Every summer.
Does dessert drown people? Obviously not:
SUMMER causes both — a third factor.
Correlation is a clue, never a verdict.

steps.title: Hunt the third factor
steps.prompt: Countries with more TVs per person live longer. Because…
options: wealth buys both TVs and healthcare
steps.explanation: A classic third factor: national wealth drives both statistics. (The arrow-backwards option at least made you check!)

steps.title: Sample bias
beats: Poll gym members: 'everyone exercises!'
Poll your followers: 'everyone loves me!'
The sample must LOOK LIKE the population.
And size can't fix bias — 2.4 million wrong answers once called an election 19 points off.

steps.title: Pictures that lie
beats: Bars of 100 vs 102 on an axis from 98: looks DOUBLE.
Same data from 0: two nearly equal bars. Honest.
Cropped axes, cherry-picked windows, missing context —
always read the numbers ON the axes first.

steps.title: The 2% cliff
steps.prompt: Sales rose 100 → 102. On a bar chart starting at 98, the second bar LOOKS…
options: twice as tall — while the real growth is 2%
steps.explanation: Bar heights above the crop: 2 vs 4 units — visually doubled. Real change: $\tfrac{2}{100} = 2\%$.

examples.prompt: Ad: 'Users lost UP TO 10 kg!' What do the five questions reveal to ask?
steps: 'Up to' = the single best case, not typical.
Who was sampled? Versus what control?
Where's the MEDIAN loss and the spread?
An honest claim quotes center + spread; this quotes the maximum.

examples.prompt: Hospital A has higher death rates than B. A is a top referral center for the sickest cases. Fair comparison?
steps: No — the SAMPLES differ: A treats harder cases.
(This reversal even has a name: Simpson's paradox.)

steps.title: Skeptic drills
steps.intro: Third factor? Sample? Axes? Center? Versus what?

problems.prompt: 'People who take vitamins are healthier.' The strongest objection is…
options: vitamin-takers may differ in other habits too
problems.explanation: Health-conscious people exercise, eat better AND take vitamins — the habits are the third factor. (Only randomized trials cut this knot.)

problems.prompt: An online poll on a gaming site: '92% play games daily.' The flaw is…
options: the percentage is too high to be real
problems.explanation: Perfect illustration of sampling bias: the venue pre-selects the answer.

problems.prompt: A claim quotes only the mean of a very skewed dataset. Your first request:
problems.explanation: Center choice + spread — this unit's toolkit — is exactly what skew-hiders omit.

steps.title: The book that started the fight
steps.body: Darrell Huff's 'How to Lie with Statistics' (1954) — a slim illustrated guide to every trick in this lesson — became the best-selling statistics book in history. Its lesson aged perfectly: the tricks haven't changed, only the charts got interactive. You now own both sides: how to compute honestly, and how to catch those who don't.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Correlation ≠ causation: hunt third factors and reversed arrows.
Samples must represent; size never fixes bias.
Read axes; demand the median AND the spread for skewed claims.

practice.statement: Add 100 to that list. Which center moves more?
practice.solution: Mean: $6 \to \tfrac{130}{6} \approx 21.7$; median: $6 \to 6.5$. The mean, by far.

practice.statement: What percent of normal data lies above $\mu + \sigma$?
Test $\mu = 55, \sigma = 8$: what score is the 97.5th percentile?
Ice cream ↔ sunburn correlation: name the third factor and the fix.
practice.solution: Sunny weather drives both; compare within same-weather days (control for it).

testYourself.solution: Mean $\tfrac{180}{6} = 30$; sorted middle pair $15, 18$: median $16.5$. Skew: report the median.

testYourself.statement: Physics: 82 ($\mu = 70, \sigma = 8$). Chemistry: 90 ($\mu = 80, \sigma = 10$). Which performance is stronger?
testYourself.solution: Physics $z = 1.5$; chemistry $z = 1$. Physics — despite fewer raw points.

testYourself.statement: Lifetimes $\mu = 800$ h, $\sigma = 50$: what percent last 700–900 hours?
What percent of normal data lies between $\mu$ and $\mu + 2\sigma$?
A recruiter quotes mean salary 80k where the median is 44k. What's happening, and which figure should a candidate trust?
testYourself.solution: Right skew — executive salaries drag the mean. Trust the median (~the typical offer).
